statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
The median of the sequence $ [s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k] $ is defined as the element that appears at position $ \lfloor \frac{k+1}{2} \rfloor $ when the sequence is sorted in non-decreasing order. For example, the median of the sequence $ [4, 5, 6, 1, 2, 2] $ is $ 2 $ ; the median of the sequence $ [3, 6, 3, 4, 5] $ is $ 4 $ .

You are given two arrays $ [a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n] $ and $ [b_1, b_2, \dots, b_m] $ , sorted in non-decreasing order. Both arrays have odd lengths.

In one operation, you can do the following:

- choose one of the arrays and mark an odd number of elements in it;
- calculate $ x $ — the median of the marked elements;
- remove all marked elements;
- insert $ x $ into any position of the array on which the operation was performed.

Your task is to determine whether it is possible to make the arrays equal.

Input Format
The first line contains one integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ) — the number of test cases.

Each test case consists of three lines:

- the first line contains two integers $ n $ and $ m $ ( $ 1 \le n, m \le 3 \cdot 10^5 $ );
- the second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n $ ( $ 1 \le a_1 \le a_2 \le \dots \le a_n \le 10^9 $ );
- the third line contains $ m $ integers $ b_1, b_2, \dots, b_m $ ( $ 1 \le b_1 \le b_2 \le \dots \le b_m \le 10^9 $ ).

Additional constraints on the input:

- the sum of $ (n+m) $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 3 \cdot 10^5 $ ;
- in every test case, $ n \bmod 2 = 1 $ and $ m \bmod 2 = 1 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output YES if it is possible to make the arrays equal, or NO if it is not possible.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3
5 3
1 2 3 4 5
1 3 7
3 5
11 17 19
19 20 26 29 37
1 7
11
1 2 7 9 11 15 17
Output
YES
NO
YES

Note
In the first example, the arrays can be made equal by applying the following sequence of operations:

1. for the array $ a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] $ , mark the $ 2 $ -nd, $ 4 $ -th, and $ 5 $ -th elements. Their median is $ 4 $ . We insert it at the end of the array, resulting in $ a = [1, 3, 4] $ ;
2. for the array $ b = [1, 3, 7] $ , mark all elements. Their median is $ 3 $ . We get the array $ b = [3] $ ;
3. for the array $ a = [1, 3, 4] $ , mark all elements. Their median is $ 3 $ . We get the array $ a = [3] $ ;
4. now the arrays $ a $ and $ b $ are equal.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2199D - Two Arrays
The first observation is as follows: if it is possible to make two arrays identical, then we can subsequently apply the same operations to them until their sizes become equal to $$$1$$$. Therefore, let's check if there exists some number $$$x$$$ to which both arrays can be transformed.
Let's try to understand what this $$$x$$$ could be for each array. If the array already has length $$$1$$$, then the only element of this array is the only possible value for $$$x$$$.
Otherwise, let's determine which elements of the array can be $$$x$$$ and which cannot. The first and last elements cannot be $$$x$$$, since in order to reduce the size to $$$1$$$, each element must participate in at least one operation; but as soon as we use the minimum or maximum in an operation, they will be removed.
Let's prove that any other element, except for the first or last, can be the only remaining one. Suppose we want to leave only the $$$i$$$-th element in the end. If there are an equal number of elements on the left and right, we can use all elements in one operation, and the $$$i$$$-th will remain as a result. If, for example, there are more elements on the left, we can choose any $$$3$$$ elements from the left and remove $$$2$$$ of them. Since the parity of the number of elements on the left and right of $$$i$$$ is the same, we can perform this operation until the number of elements on the left and right of $$$i$$$ is equal. Thus, we can leave the $$$i$$$-th element as the last one.
Now we know how to determine for each array all the elements that can be the only remaining ones. Let $$$a'$$$ be the array of all elements to which we can transform $$$a$$$ ($$$b'$$$ is defined similarly). To solve the problem, it is now sufficient to check that there is at least one element that belongs to both $$$a'$$$ and $$$b'$$$. We can iterate through all elements of $$$a'$$$ and check using binary search if this element exists in $$$b'$$$; or we can convert both arrays to a
Set
and call the
intersect
method.

solutions.title: Solution (Roms)
solutions.content:
import
java
.
util
.
StringTokenizer
fun main
()
{
val t
=
readLine
()!!.
toInt
()
repeat
(
t
)
{
val st
=
StringTokenizer
(
readLine
())
st
.
nextToken
()
st
.
nextToken
()
val a
=
readLine
()!!.
split
(
" "
).
map
{
it
.
toInt
()
}
val b
=
readLine
()!!.
split
(
" "
).
map
{
it
.
toInt
()
}
val aTrimmed
=
if
(
a
.
size
>
1
)
a
.
subList
(
1
,
a
.
size
-
1
)
else
a
        val bTrimmed
=
if
(
b
.
size
>
1
)
b
.
subList
(
1
,
b
.
size
-
1
)
else
b

        val ok
=
aTrimmed
.
toSet
().
intersect
(
bTrimmed
.
toSet
()).
isNotEmpty
()
println
(
if
(
ok
)
"Yes"
else
"No"
)
}
}
